summary: Long-only mutual funds (Fidelity, Baillie Gifford, Capital Research, Wellington, T. Rowe) are at or near their self-imposed private allocation caps (3-7%), creating a massive wave of pent-up demand that will be unleashed when large private companies go public and exit the illiquid bucket.
summary_extended: Baker argues that long-only mutual funds such as Fidelity, Baillie Gifford, Capital Research, Wellington, and T. Rowe Price have hit or are approaching their self-imposed private allocation caps of 3-7% of AUM — well below the SEC-permitted ceiling of 15% — creating a structural demand vacuum in late-stage tech equities. The mechanism is straightforward: these funds cannot add new private positions without selling existing ones, as evidenced by Baillie Gifford being forced to sell its SpaceX stake due to capacity constraints. When large private companies like SpaceX, Anthropic, and Anduril eventually IPO and lock-up periods expire, these funds will be able to re-enter as public equity buyers, unleashing what Baker describes as 'hundreds of billions of dollars of new late-stage demand' back into the market. Baker frames this as a major structural catalyst for late-stage tech equities, not a company-specific thesis, with the investment implication being that the IPO pipeline from large private tech companies will be met with unusually strong institutional demand once liquidity constraints are removed.
detail: Long-only mutual funds can allocate up to 15% of AUM to privates per SEC rules but self-cap at 3-7%. Baillie Gifford was forced to sell SpaceX last year due to regulatory/capacity constraints. When these companies IPO and lock-ups expire, hundreds of billions in new demand will re-enter the market. Baker frames this as a major structural catalyst for late-stage tech equities.
confidence_evidence: Baker presents this as a specific, named structural dynamic with named institutions and a clear mechanism. Described as 'hundreds of billions of dollars of new late stage demand.'
quote_fragment: hundreds of billions of dollars of new late stage demand coming back to the market

ticker_audit.reasoning: SpaceX is private and not US-listed; it was cited only as an illustrative example of a company that caused Baillie Gifford to hit its cap, not as an investable subject or beneficiary of this thesis. No actionable equity exposure exists.

Anthropic is private and not US-listed; it was mentioned as part of the broader pipeline of large private companies that could eventually IPO, but there is no current investable equity and the thesis does not make it an unambiguous specific beneficiary over other private companies.
Anduril is private and not US-listed; same reasoning as Anthropic — mentioned as part of the general IPO pipeline narrative but not a specific investable beneficiary at this time.
